schools.summary: Gill: the five days reckoned from Paul's coming to Caesarea; Ananias the high priest came down with the elders (the sanhedrim) and a hired orator, not merely as accusers ordered by the captain but willingly, to vindicate themselves and their people lest they fall under the governor's displeasure for encouraging tumults (the high priest conscious he had acted illegally); the eagerness of the Jewish leaders to prosecute one prisoner in person (the depth of their enmity, the persistent malice), the hiring of Tertullus (the eloquence of man arrayed against the truth, the self-interest mingled with the malice).

themes: Ananias the high priest descended with the elders
the orator Tertullus
the leaders pursuing their enmity
the hired orator against Paul

schools.summary: Barnes: 'we enjoy great quietness' said in the customary style of flatterers and orators to conciliate the governor's favour, strikingly in contrast with Paul's more honest, straightforward introduction (v.10); though said for flattery and though Felix was an unjust ruler, the orator opened with praise; the flattering exordium (the captatio benevolentiae) covering the weakness of the case (Felix in fact unjust and cruel, vv.25-27, the praise false); the corruption of pleading by flattery (the appeal to the judge's vanity, the truth not needing such arts), contrasted with the apostle's honesty.

themes: by thee we enjoy great quietness
worthy deeds... by thy providence
the flattering exordium
contrasted with Paul's honesty

schools.summary: Barnes paraphrases: 'we admit it is owing to your vigilance, and we accept your interposition to promote peace, with gratitude... we do not commend you merely when present'; Gill: the Jews professed to acknowledge the favours under his government; the lavish, extravagant flattery ('always, and in all places... with all thankfulness,' the superlatives heaped to win the judge) — the falseness of the profession (the Jews not in truth loving the cruel, unjust Felix, the hollow praise), the art that flatters before it accuses; the title 'most noble Felix' calculated to please, contrasted with Paul's brief honest courtesy (v.10).

themes: we accept it always, and in all places
most noble Felix, with all thankfulness
the lavish flattery
conciliation of the judge

schools.summary: Clarke: Tertullus suggests he would not trespass longer on the governor's time, nor on his modesty by enumerating his deeds, and asks him to hear their complaint of his clemency — 'the whole of this exordium was artful enough'; Barnes: he would not be tedious with an introduction and commendation; the artful transition from flattery to the charge (the practiced structure of the rhetorician — flattering exordium, modest transition, accusation to follow), the appeal to Felix's 'clemency' (the flattery continued), the calculated brevity ('a few words'); the orator's craft covering the weakness of an unjust cause.

themes: that I be not further tedious
hear us of thy clemency a few words
the artful transition
the orator's craft

schools.summary: Gill: 'pestilent fellow' (the word signifying the pest/plague itself), usual with Roman orators to call a wicked man the 'pest' of the state; Barnes: the word applied to one who corrupts others or excites sedition; the threefold charge — a plague, a mover of sedition among all the Jews throughout the world (the political charge calculated to alarm the Roman governor, who feared sedition), a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes (the religious charge, the Christians contemptuously named after Nazareth, John 1:46); the political framing of a religious dispute (the charges false, the apostle slandered as a seditious plague).

themes: a pestilent fellow, a mover of sedition
a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes
the threefold charge
the political framing of the accusation

schools.summary: Gill: the charge of profaning the temple (introducing a Greek) only a supposition and conjecture of the Asiatic Jews, false and groundless; Barnes: a serious but unfounded charge arising from the calumny that he introduced Greeks into the sacred place (Acts 21:28, answered in v.18), 'would have judged' = would have condemned and punished according to their law; the false claim of lawful intent (in truth they intended not to judge but to kill him, Acts 21:31; 23:12, the pretence of legality covering the murderous intent), the orator distorting the mob violence as a lawful seizure.

themes: gone about to profane the temple
would have judged according to our law
the unfounded charge
the false claim of lawful judgment

schools.summary: Barnes: Tertullus pretends they would have judged Paul righteously had Lysias not interposed, but the truth was that without regard to law or justice they would have murdered him on the spot (Acts 21:31); Clarke: 'with great violence' rather means with an armed force, Tertullus intimating Lysias interfered contrary to law; the inversion of the facts (the captain, who rescued Paul from the murderous mob, Acts 21:32, blamed as an unlawful interferer — the deliverer made the offender), the slander of Lysias (to remove the inconvenient witness whose report, Acts 23:26-30, would clear Paul).

themes: the chief captain Lysias came upon us
took him away out of our hands
blaming the captain who prevented a murder
the inverted facts

schools.summary: Gill: Tertullus would insinuate that the captain was blameworthy for hindering a legal process and troubling the governor; Barnes: the appeal — by examining the accusers (or Paul), Felix could learn the truth of the charges; Tertullus resting his case on the judge's inquiry, falsely confident the examination would confirm the accusation (in truth it would clear Paul, as his defence and the absent witnesses show, vv.13, 19); the weak case that could not bear inquiry (the examination would expose the falsehood), the irony of the invitation (the appeal that backfired, the inquiry favouring the truth).

themes: by examining... thyself mayest take knowledge
the appeal to examination
resting the case on inquiry
false confidence the inquiry would confirm

schools.summary: Gill: Ananias and the elders agreed to what Tertullus said and confirmed it; Barnes: the Jews who accompanied Tertullus bore testimony, confirming unanimously the accusation; Robertson: 'joined in the charge' (sunepethento, a double compound, to make a joint attack); the united front of the accusers (the high priest and elders, with the orator, making a joint attack on one prisoner, the weight of numbers and authority arrayed against the truth, Psalm 94:21), the assenting to known falsehood (the murderous intent, the false temple charge), the providence overruling the united attack (Felix deferring, v.22).

themes: the Jews also assented
saying that these things were so
the united front of the accusers
numbers against the truth

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Paul's defence shows the spirit of wisdom and holiness and the fulfilment of Christ's promise (Matthew 10:19-20) that his followers before governors should be given what to speak; though Tertullus said provoking things, Paul did not interrupt him; Paul opening not with flattery but with a brief, truthful acknowledgment of Felix's long experience as judge (the honest opening, the truth not extravagant praise, contrasted with the orator's flattery), the patience that hears before it answers, the cheerful confidence of the innocent.

themes: thou hast been of many years a judge
I do the more cheerfully answer
Paul's honest opening
the spirit of wisdom given by Christ

schools.summary: Barnes: Felix could ascertain by proper testimony that Paul had been but twelve days in Jerusalem and had been orderly and peaceable; the first part of Paul's defence (vv.11-13) an express denial of the charges; Paul pointing out he had been only twelve days in Jerusalem and came to worship (too short a time and too pious a purpose to have raised the sedition 'among all the Jews throughout the world' charged, v.5) — the appeal to the verifiable facts refuting the wild accusation (the truth against the distortion, the fact against the flourish, the honest defence resting on evidence).

themes: but twelve days since I went up to Jerusalem
for to worship
the appeal to verifiable facts
refuting the charge of sedition

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was so far from moving sedition that he never even entered into conversation with the people on any subject; true, he was found in the temple, but not disputing — purifying himself according to the law; Barnes: this meets the first charge of Tertullus; the comprehensive denial (not found disputing or raising the people in the temple, synagogues, or city) — the plain refutation of the sedition charged (the peaceable, quiet conduct, the absence of any tumult), the challenge to the accusers to produce any instance (the wild charge against the quiet fact, the innocence plain).

themes: neither found me... disputing
neither raising up the people
the denial of disturbance
the peaceable conduct

schools.summary: Gill: the charges (a pestilent fellow, a mover of sedition, a profaner of the temple) they could not set forth in any clear light, nor bring testimonies to demonstrate; it lay upon them to prove them, or he ought to be acquitted — 'this was challenging and defying them to make good their assertions'; Barnes: Paul boldly challenges investigation; the bold challenge of the innocent (the burden of proof unmet, the accusers unable to demonstrate the charge, the charges unproven so the accusation falls), the principle of justice (the accuser must prove the charge, the accused not condemned without proof).

themes: neither can they prove the things
the challenge to prove
the want of proof
the bold confidence of the innocent

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul does not stoop to the contempt in the word 'Nazarenes' (v.5), being engaged in more important business; he admits he belonged to that 'way' (sect), neither denying nor disposed to deny he was a Christian, and maintains the continuity; Paul openly confessing the Way (called 'heresy'/a sect by his accusers) as the worship of the God of the fathers, believing all written in the law and the prophets — Christianity not a new religion but the fulfilment of the Scriptures (the continuity with Israel's faith, Acts 10:43), the bold confession unashamed (Romans 1:16), the charge of heresy refuted.

debates.question: Is the Way ('called heresy') a departure from Israel's faith?

sides.position: No — it is the worship of the God of the fathers, believing all the law and prophets; Christianity the fulfilment of the Scriptures, not a new religion

themes: after the way which they call heresy, so worship I the God of my fathers
believing all things written in the law and prophets
the confession of the Way
continuity with Israel's faith

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul's hope of the resurrection arises from the promises of God; 'which they themselves also allow' = the Pharisees, who held this same cardinal point (as they held it, they could not arraign him for holding it); the resurrection of 'both the just and unjust,' of all the race; Gill: the hope of an interest in God and eternal life; Paul professing the resurrection hope (the orthodox Jewish hope held by the Pharisees) — vindicating his orthodoxy (the Way no heresy but the true Jewish hope), the resurrection of the just to life and the unjust to judgment (John 5:29), the hope grounded toward God.

themes: a resurrection of the dead, both of the just and unjust
which they themselves also allow
the resurrection hope
the common ground vindicating his orthodoxy

schools.summary: Gill: because of the doctrine of the resurrection (which promotes a holy life), Paul studied, laboured, and bent himself, employing his thought, care, and time; Barnes: 'I exercise myself' (askō) = 'I make it my constant aim and endeavour,' Paul often appealing to his conscientiousness as the leading habit of his life; Paul labouring to keep a conscience void of offence toward God (piety, faith) and men (justice, love) — the holy, conscientious life as the fruit of the resurrection hope (the doctrine according to godliness, the good conscience the mark of the believer, 1 Timothy 1:5,19), the integrity refuting the charge.

themes: a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men
herein do I exercise myself
the good conscience
the fruit of the resurrection hope

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul begins to reply to the charge of profaning the temple — his design in coming was to bring needed aid in a time of distress (the collection, Romans 15:25-26); absurd to suppose one who came to bring alms and offerings came to violate the temple's customs; Gill: about twenty-five years since his conversion, most spent among the Gentiles; Paul's charitable, pious purpose (alms to his nation, the collection for the poor saints, a labour of love; offerings, to worship and fulfil his vow, v.18) refuting the charge (the love against the slander of enmity, the piety against the charge of profaning).

themes: I came to bring alms to my nation, and offerings
the charitable purpose
refuting the charge of profaning the temple
the labour of love

schools.summary: Clarke: the Asian Jews who stirred up the persecution found Paul purified in the temple, regularly performing his vow, giving no cause for suspicion (making no tumult, nor having a number of people by whom he could accomplish a seditious purpose); Barnes: he was found engaged in the sacred service of completing his vow, in a quiet and peaceful manner; Paul found purified, without crowd or tumult — the opposite of the sedition charged (the devout, peaceable conduct against the accusation), the irony (the accusers' own observation refuting their charge), the tumult raised by the accusers themselves (Acts 21:27-30), not Paul.

themes: found me purified in the temple
neither with multitude, nor with tumult
the peaceable devout conduct
the opposite of the sedition charged

schools.summary: Barnes: the Asian Jews were the proper witnesses (they alone saw him in the temple and raised the charge); as they stayed away, it showed they were not prepared to undergo a strict examination; Paul justly complains that the very persons who alone could testify against him were absent (showing no well-founded charge), and being absent, the charge ought to be dismissed; the fatal flaw in the prosecution (the case lacking its essential witnesses, the absence itself a confession of weakness), the injustice of being accused by those who could not testify while others pressed on hearsay.

themes: who ought to have been here before thee
the absent Asian Jews
the true witnesses missing
the flaw in the prosecution

schools.summary: Barnes: since the Asian Jews were not present to witness about the temple, Paul calls on those present (the high priest and elders) to bear witness, if they can, to any improper conduct while he stood before the sanhedrim (Acts 23:1-10) — a bold appeal showing his full consciousness of innocence; he admits they were competent to testify to his conduct there; the bold challenge defying the accusers to name any 'evil doing' (the clear conscience emboldening it, the innocence confident), the unanswerable challenge (the accusers could name only the resurrection, v.21, no crime), the wisdom of the precise appeal to competent witnesses.

themes: let these same here say, if they have found any evil doing
while I stood before the council
the challenge to the present accusers
the bold appeal of innocence

schools.summary: Gill: Paul did not own this an evil in him, but that it was the only thing any of them (a part, the Sadducees) could judge to be evil, the resurrection the one point; Barnes: this the main thing Paul insisted on before the council, he calling them to show if they could that he was wrong, not a confession of wrong but the one ground of accusation; the only charge his cry about the resurrection (Acts 23:6) — no crime but the orthodox hope (v.15), the accusation reduced to a point of doctrine (the religious dispute the true issue, the political charges a pretext), the division it exposed between Pharisees and Sadducees.

themes: except it be for this one voice
touching the resurrection... I am called in question
the resurrection the only charge
the religious dispute the true ground

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Felix put them off on purpose, not to learn but to get rid of the Jews; on their account he did not like to let Paul go, to punish him was not possible (too barefaced) — so entirely did he acquit Paul of the charges; Felix, having more perfect knowledge of the Way (perhaps from Drusilla, v.24), deferring the case until Lysias should come — the injustice of delay (convinced of Paul's innocence yet unwilling to offend the Jews by acquitting him), the fear of man perverting justice (Proverbs 29:25), the pretext of waiting for Lysias (whose report, Acts 23:26-30, already cleared Paul).

themes: he deferred them
when Lysias shall come down
convinced of innocence yet fearing the Jews
the injustice of delay

schools.summary: Barnes: Felix disposed to show Paul all favours consistent with his safe keeping, esteeming him a persecuted man, regarding the charges as malicious (perhaps detaining him to gratify the Jews and hoping for money); Chrysostom: by giving him liberty and allowing his friends to minister, Felix so entirely acquitted him of the charges; the mild custody (liberty, free access to friends) tacitly acknowledging Paul's innocence (the favour confessing the case had no merit), the inconsistency of Felix (acknowledging innocence yet withholding justice to please the Jews), the providence softening the confinement.

themes: to let him have liberty
forbid none of his acquaintance to minister
the mild custody
the tacit acknowledgment of innocence

schools.summary: Gill and Barnes: Drusilla the daughter of Herod Agrippa the elder, a Jewess, whom Felix had lured from her husband Azizus (king of Emesa) to marry him (their union itself adulterous, the corrupt pair); Felix sending for Paul to hear him concerning the faith in Christ — the apostle given opportunity to preach Christ to the rulers (the providence turning the confinement to a pulpit, Matthew 10:18, the prisoner the preacher), the curiosity that brought them (Drusilla a Jewess, Felix knowing of the gospel, v.22) the hearing not yet faith, the faithful preaching to come (v.25).

themes: Felix came with his wife Drusilla
heard him concerning the faith in Christ
the opportunity to preach to the rulers
the corrupt pair who heard

schools.summary: Gill: Paul reasoned of righteousness (justice between man and man, greatly wanting in the cruel, unjust Felix), temperance (against his adulterous union with Drusilla), and judgment to come — falling pointedly on the sins of his hearers; the faithful, searching preaching (the apostle not flattering but reasoning of the very truths that condemned them, fearless before the great); Felix trembling (convicted, the conscience pierced) yet putting it off to 'a convenient season' — the tragedy of conviction resisted and deferred (the season that never came, Hebrews 3:15; 2 Corinthians 6:2, the peril of delay).

themes: reasoned of righteousness, temperance, and judgment to come
Felix trembled
when I have a convenient season
conviction resisted and postponed

schools.summary: Gill: Felix observed from Paul's defence that he came to bring alms, and from the indictment that he was a man of consequence with a large following, hoping Paul or his friends would supply money for his freedom (perhaps why he allowed free access); Barnes: the hope to weary Paul into purchasing his freedom; Felix, who had trembled under the word (v.25), now seeking gain from the prisoner (the covetousness perverting justice, the venal governor selling freedom for a bribe, 1 Timothy 6:10), the frequent interviews driven by greed; the hardening after the trembling (the conviction followed not by repentance but by covetousness), the contrast with Paul's integrity.

themes: he hoped that money should have been given him
sent for him the oftener
the hope of a bribe
covetousness perverting justice

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul unjustly detained two years, Felix's hope to weary him into purchasing his freedom; 'Felix, willing to shew the Jews a pleasure' = desirous of pleasing them even at the expense of justice, leaving Paul bound to conciliate the Jews and prevent them accusing him for the evils of his administration; Gill: the two years of Paul's confinement at Caesarea; the final injustice of the man-pleasing judge (sacrificing the innocent to win favour, John 12:43, the self-interest of protecting himself before the emperor), the providence preserving Paul through the unjust detention (kept for further service, Acts 27).

themes: after two years Porcius Festus came
Felix... left Paul bound
willing to shew the Jews a pleasure
the injustice of the man-pleasing judge
